Sirmauri line-break analysis workbench

Side-by-side manuscript images, Devanagari/Roman readings, tentative glosses, and line-break evidence. The proposal’s own proposed line-break property table is treated as a hypothesis, not as evidence.

AnalysisMethodologyFigure 2Figure 4Figure 6More manuscriptsSources

Analysis: Unicode-facing note

Recommendation from the current evidence: keep the default conservative for encoded Sirmauri. Treat letters as ordinary alphabetic characters for modern spaced text, keep dependent vowel signs and other combining marks with their base letters, and do not create default breaks inside an akshara/grapheme cluster. The primary manuscripts strongly show scribal line filling of weakly spaced or unspaced text streams, but the available readings do not yet prove that arbitrary lexical word-internal breaks are expected in modern plain text.

There are two different problems here. A layout-history question asks how scribes filled ruled manuscript lines. For that, Figures 6–17 are quite strong: many lines end at a physical rule or margin, without visible hyphenation or continuation marks. A Unicode default behavior question asks whether encoded text should automatically allow breaks inside ordinary Sirmauri words. For that, the evidence is weaker, because most primary manuscripts still lack secure word segmentation. The safest interpretation is: historical Sirmauri manuscripts can be diplomatically transcribed as unspaced or weakly spaced streams, but automatic intrastream wrapping should be handled by explicit spaces, ZWSP, soft hyphen, or higher-level tailoring rather than by a default break between every pair of letters.

Evidence tierWhat it showsUnicode-facing interpretation
Figure 2: Grierson SirmauriMostly word/sentence boundary line ends; one split inside khoi-khanday-ro, but at a morpheme boundary.Does not justify arbitrary word-internal default breaks. It supports keeping clusters intact and treating manual/semantic boundaries separately.
Figure 4: Grierson JaunsariDensely written visual lines; full visible-page reading included here, but Grierson explicitly warns the Jaunsari Sirmauri-character texts are carelessly written.Useful as line-layout evidence, weaker as normative Sirmauri evidence or fine segmentation evidence.
Figures 6–12: land grantsPrimary administrative manuscripts, ruled or margin-constrained, generally weakly spaced/unspaced.Strong evidence that historical scribal layout allowed physical line filling; still not enough to set a default that breaks ordinary encoded words automatically.
Figures 13–17 and selected later pagesRitual/sāñcā manuscripts with punctuation, section labels, and ruled lines. Tables/diagrams are excluded.Useful for manuscript wrapping and punctuation behavior; section signs must not be confused with line-break opportunities.

Practical UTC question: if the encoded corpus is expected to represent modern typed Sirmauri with spaces, ordinary alphabetic behavior is appropriate. If the encoded corpus is expected to preserve manuscripts without inserting spaces, then applications will need a manuscript-transcription policy: either add explicit break controls during transcription or tailor line breaking for that corpus. The attestations do not support breaking between a consonant and its dependent vowel sign, halanta, nukta, anusvāra, or medial-ra sign.

Methodology notes

I used Grierson only where he supplies an external transliteration and translation for Figures 2 and 4. The Grierson pages are useful for mapping the text, but the Jaunsari specimen is lower-trust for orthographic details because Grierson says the Sirmauri-character versions were carelessly written and that dots/dashes were often omitted or added. Primary manuscript figures are therefore weighted more heavily for layout, but less heavily for lexical segmentation until a fluent reader confirms the word divisions.

A “Grierson-hyphenated word-like unit” is a unit joined by hyphens in Grierson’s romanization. It may combine a stem with case/postpositions, participial material, or clitic-like elements. It is useful because it gives a segmentation hypothesis from an external source, but it is not automatically the same thing as a modern orthographic word. Thus the Figure 2 split khoi-khanday- / ro is marked MB: it is inside a Grierson-hyphenated unit, but at a morpheme boundary, not an arbitrary letter break.

high-confidence readingguess / context-supportedunread or ambiguousLB MB VL No cluster split

Figure 2 Grierson Sirmauri specimen

controlled evidence Corrected Sirmauri/Dharthi opening; token highlighting links Roman, Devanagari, and gloss.
Best controlled token-level evidence. Most line ends align with word/sentence boundaries; the khoi-khanday-/ro split is the only observed split inside a Grierson-hyphenated unit and is morpheme-boundary evidence, not evidence for arbitrary intraword breaking.
Visual lineRomanDevanagariGlossBreak
1Eki-jone-re du bete thiye.एकि-जोने-रे दू बेटे थिये।of a certain man two sons wereLB
2Kanchhe-bete apne-baw-khe bolo,कंछे-बेटे अपने-बाव-खे बोलो,younger son to his father saidLB
3bapu, mere-banda hisab ma-khe de.बापु, मेरे-बंडा हिसाब मा-खे दे।father my portion share to me giveLB
4Teniye tini-khe hisab bande-diya.तेनिये तिनी-खे हिसाब बंडे-दिया।by him to them share was dividedLB
5Thore-dine-pachhe kanchhe-bete apnaथोरे-दिने-पछे कंछे-बेटे अपनाafter a few days younger son his ownLB
6banda hisab lo-re dur-deso-kheबंडा हिसाब लो-रे दूर-देसो-खेportion share having taken to a far countryLB
7do-gowa, hor apna mal khoi-khanday-दो-गोवा, होर अपना माल खोइ-खंडय-went away and his own property lost/squandered-MB
8ro gaway-diya. Jabe sara malरो गवाय-दिया। जबे सारा मालhaving was wasted when all propertyMB
9latay-hota, tabe tesi-deso-da kalलटाय-होता, तबे तेसी-देसो-दा कालwas spent then in that country famineLB

Figure 4 Grierson Jaunsari specimen, full visible-page pass

weak/moderate Rows are grouped by visible text line in the figure image, not by Grierson’s printed translation/gloss line divisions.
Use with caution. This is a completed first-pass reading of the visible Figure 4 page. It normalizes Grierson’s transliteration into a readable Roman form and a Devanagari proxy. The final visible line is marked less secure because it appears near the Grierson page transition. The Devanagari is a phonetic/Indic-readable normalization, not a claim that the Sirmauri glyphs have been diplomatically encoded letter-for-letter.
Visible lineRomanized readingDevanagari readingTranslation / line noteBreak
1Eko-ke dui bete the. Tihu-mujhi joja kanchha thaएको-के दुई बेटे थे। तिहु-मुझि जोजा कंछा थाOf a certain man there were two sons; among them the younger was…
Line matching is fairly secure from the opening of Grierson’s transliteration.

2tine apne-baba-kh bolu je, “baba, jo-kichh dhan-taka o,तिने अपने-बाबा-ख बोलु जे, “बाबा, जो-किछ धन-टका ओ,he said to his father, “Father, whatever wealth/money there is…”
Visible line appears to end after the existential o “is”.
LB?
3tethu-mujhi jo-kichh more-bato-ko, so mu-kh de.” Tabi tineतेथु-मुझि जो-किछ मोरे-बाटो-को, सो मु-ख दे।” तबी तिने“from that, whatever is my share, give that to me.” Then he…
Line boundary follows a complete quoted request plus continuation.
LB?
4jo-kichh tho, so tiho-kh bati-dino. Thore-duso-mujh joja kanchhaजो-किछ थो, सो तिहो-ख बाटि-दिनो। थोरे-दुसो-मुझ जोजा कंछाdivided whatever there was among them. After a few days, the younger…
Line includes a sentence boundary but continues into the next clause.
VL
5beta tha, tine sabh-apne-bato-ko mal-matah katho-karo koroबेटा था, तिने सभ-अपने-बाटो-को माल-मातह काठो-कारो कोरोson gathered together all the property of his own share…
Break at a visible line end; linguistic boundary not proven.
VL
6(aur) pardeso-kh nutha, aur tainke jaria-mejh apni mal-matah(होर) परदेसो-ख नुठा, होर तैंके जारिया-मेज्ह अपनी माल-मातहand went to another/far country, and there in dissipation his property…
Break after a complete object phrase; likely a word boundary if the line match is right.
LB?
7sab dini-udai. Jab tes-bher kichh-nu riho aurसब दिनी-उदाई। जब तेस-भेर किछ-नु रिहो होरwas all squandered away. When nothing remained with him and…
Line end is a clause-continuation point after aur “and”.
VL
8tainke kar pura, so munika garib goa. Tabi so jo tainko-keतैंके कर पूरा, सो मुनिका गरीब गोआ। तबी सो जो तैंको-केa famine occurred there; he became entirely poor. Then the one who was of that place…
Visible line break likely follows a postpositional/case-marked phrase.
VL
9the, tihe-mujhi eko-ke uthu renda laga, jineथे, तिहे-मुझि एको-के ऊथु रेंदा लगा, जिनेamong them, he began to stay with one; by whom…
Line end at a relative-pronoun continuation point.
VL
10tasu-kh apne-segaru charne-kh dokhri-peda bheja, aur so tike-तसु-ख अपने-सेगरु चरने-ख डोखरी-पेदा भेजा, होर सो तिके-he was sent to the fields to feed that person’s pigs; and he those…
This row is a possible hyphenated/continued form from Grierson’s lineation, not necessarily a manuscript hyphen.
VL
11bokute-kh khane seche-tha jihu-kh sugur khao-the; teso-kh sejeबोकुते-ख खाने सेचे-था जिहु-ख सुगुर खाओ-थे; तेसो-ख सेजेhusks he wished to eat, which the pigs were eating; to him those…
Visible line end seems to continue the object/complement into the next line.
VL
12bi koe ne tha dei. Tabi teso-kh such ai je, “mere-baba-keबि कोए ने था देई। तबी तेसो-ख सूच आई जे, “मेरे-बाबा-केalso no one was giving. Then sense came to him: “At my father’s…”
Sentence boundary followed by new quoted thought on the same visual line.
VL
13uthu to kochhe-i pet bhare, or kau bhukha moru. Hau apne-ऊथु तो कोछे-ई पेट भरे, ओर कउ भूखा मोरु। हउ अपने-place, so many bellies are filled, and I die hungry. I my own…
The apne- split follows Grierson’s hyphenation before baba-bher; count as unclassified here, not manuscript-internal proof.
VL
14baba-bher jama, or tese-kh bolma je, “baba, me surugo-koबाबा-भेर जमा, ओर तसे-ख बोलमा जे, “बाबा, मे सुरुगो-कोfather-near will go, and I will say to him, “Father, I against heaven…”
Visible line break after a genitival/postpositional phrase.
VL
15aur tere samne pap kiya-o. Hau tere-bete-maphak athi-na. Mu-kh biहोर तेरे सामने पाप किया-ओ। हउ तेरे-बेटे-माफक अथी-ना। मु-ख बिand before you have sinned. I am not like your son. Me also…
Line end after bi “also”; syntactically continued.
VL
16jaise tere or hari-pari o tosei rakh.”जैसे तेरे ओर हरी-परी ओ तोसेई रख।”keep me just as one of your other servants.”
Completes the quoted thought; likely line/sentence boundary.
LB?
17Tabio ujhuna ujhuna, or apne-baba-bhero-kh nutha.तबिओ उझुना उझुना, ओर अपने-बाबा-भेरो-ख नुठा।Then he arose and went to his own father.
Final visible-line match is less secure: it may be the next Grierson page/page-break continuation, but it appears to fit the bottom line of the figure image.
LB?
Line-break implication for Figure 4: the page is dense and close to unspaced. The visible rows show physical line filling, but because the specimen is Jaunsari and Grierson flags carelessness in the handwritten Sirmauri-character versions, I would not use Figure 4 alone to justify default word-internal line breaks.

Figure 6 first-pass primary manuscript transcription

primary layout evidence Two land-donation records to the Shirgul Devta temple: Shamsher Prakash on the left, Jagat Prakash on the right.
Transcription attempt ignores seals, signatures, marginal marks, and mixed/non-Sirmauri scribbles except where they help identify a Sirmauri line.
Figure 6 transcription status: scaffolding pass only. Guessed spaces are for readability and are not counted as word-boundary evidence.

Left record: Shamsher Prakash side

Text lineDevanagari reading attemptRomanization attemptTranslation / parsing noteBreak
seal/nameश्री महाराज शमशेर प्रकाश जीśrī mahārāja śamśer prakāś jīIssuer/name line, supported by the caption and the visible name-like sequence.VL
L1श्री? शिरगुल/सिरगुल? देवता/देव? महाराज? …śrī? śirgul/sirgul? devtā/dev? mahārāj? …Likely beneficiary/formula for Śirgul Devta; segmentation is only a reading aid.VL
L2धरम/धर्म? … कागज/कारज? … दीयो?/दियो? dharam/dharm? … kāgaj/kāraj? … diyo?Possible grant/dharmic-donation formula, but not secure.VL
L3हमारी/हमारे? माफी? … दीनी? पट्टा? hamārī/hamāre? māfī? … dīnī? paṭṭā?Possible remission/exemption or grant-document line.VL

Right record: Jagat Prakash side

Text lineDevanagari reading attemptRomanization attemptTranslation / parsing noteBreak
seal/nameश्री महाराज जगत प्रकाश जीśrī mahārāja jagat prakāś jīIssuer/name line, supported by the caption and the visible name-like sequence.VL
R1जमा?/ग्राम? … देवा?/देवता? … राजा?/प्रजा? jamā?/grām? … devā?/devtā? … rājā?/prajā?Likely administrative/formulaic line; uncertain.VL
R2धरम/धर्म? देणो?/देना? देवो?/देवता? ग्वाल/ग्राम? को? dharam/dharm? deṇo?/denā? devo?/devtā? gwāl/grām? ko?Looks like a donation/grant phrase, but several letters remain ambiguous.VL
R3धर्मां?/धर्मार्थ? श्री? … राज/रघ? … dharmā̃?/dharmārth? śrī? … rāj/ragh? …Possible “for religious purpose / deity” formula.VL
R4करना?/करमा? … देओ?/दियो? पट्टा?/पत्तर? …karnā?/karmā? … deo?/diyo? paṭṭā?/pattar? …Likely closing/action line of the grant; too uncertain for word-boundary analysis.VL
Line-break implication for Figure 6: both records show line filling along a physical rule/margin, without visible hyphenation or continuation marks. This is good primary evidence for scribal line filling, but not yet a specific default line-break rule for ordinary encoded words.

More manuscript and inscription evidence

These cards focus on running manuscript or inscriptional Sirmauri/Jaunsari text. Alphabet charts, comparison charts, newspaper material, and table/diagram-only subfigures are omitted. The cards are primarily for line-break/layout analysis, not full transcription.

Figure 7 — Karma Prakash land-donation record — strong primary layout evidence

Text type: administrative land grant

  • Two record blocks with seals/signature marks and ruled writing zones.
  • The Sirmauri running text is weakly spaced and is guided by physical rules/margins.
  • Line endings occur at the ruled/marginal edge without a visible hyphen or continuation convention.

Line-break implication: Useful for establishing that the manuscript practice fills a ruled text stream, but insufficient by itself for lexical word-internal break defaults.

VL No cluster split

Figure 8 — Fateh Prakash land-donation record — moderate primary layout evidence

Text type: administrative land grant with rotated/lateral text

  • Horizontal text block and a separate rotated/lower text block are visible.
  • The writing area, not clear word spacing, appears to determine most line ends.
  • The image is faint and mixed-layout, so it is weaker than Figures 9–12 for fine segmentation.

Line-break implication: Supports layout-constrained wrapping; not counted for word-boundary classification.

VL No cluster split

Figure 9 — Raghbir Prakash II land-donation record — strong primary layout evidence

Text type: administrative land grant

  • Clearer ruled lines than Figure 8; a long central text block with little reliable word spacing.
  • Line ends are flush with the ruled writing area.
  • No visible hyphenation or special line-continuation mark is apparent.

Line-break implication: Good evidence for physical line filling in manuscript practice.

VL No cluster split

Figure 10 — Raghbir Prakash II land-donation record — strong primary layout evidence

Text type: administrative land grant

  • A long, narrow Sirmauri text column with many ruled lines.
  • Running text appears effectively continuous; visible spaces are not systematic enough to classify word boundaries.
  • Line endings are imposed by the column width.

Line-break implication: Among the stronger primary examples for unspaced/weakly spaced line filling.

VL No cluster split

Figure 11 — Raghbir Prakash II land-donation record — strong primary layout evidence

Text type: administrative land grant

  • Similar long ruled column; left marginal/rotated material should be treated separately from the main text.
  • Main text line ends are at the physical edge and not marked with hyphens.
  • Some strokes cross rules, but those are writing/mark features, not line-break marks.

Line-break implication: Strong line-layout evidence, but not yet segmented enough for lexical break placement.

VL No cluster split

Figure 12 — land-donation record, captioned Shamsher Prakash — strong primary layout evidence

Text type: administrative land grant

  • Large seal/signature area at top; main Sirmauri text below in a ruled block.
  • The running text uses nearly full line width and shows no systematic word spacing.
  • Several line ends appear to cut the stream at the margin without visible punctuation.

Line-break implication: Supports the same primary-document pattern as Figures 6–11.

VL No cluster split

Figure 13a — Natiram Negi Jaunsari sāñcā page — strong manuscript-layout evidence

Text type: ritual/calendar text

  • Running lines with red emphasis and boxed section markers.
  • Line breaks are still strongly tied to the ruled page layout, but section labels and punctuation complicate analysis.
  • Because the variety is Jaunsari, it should be weighed separately from central Sirmauri records.

Line-break implication: Good evidence for line-constrained manuscript practice, weaker for Sirmauri-specific word segmentation.

VL No cluster split

Figure 13b — Natiram Negi Jaunsari sāñcā page — strong manuscript-layout evidence

Text type: ritual/calendar text

  • Boxed/colored labels interrupt the running text stream.
  • The line grid is clear, and text often continues across lines without visible word-spacing structure.
  • Punctuation/sectioning is frequent enough that line ends must be distinguished from section boundaries.

Line-break implication: Useful for line layout and punctuation behavior, not yet for default word-internal wrapping.

VL No cluster split

Figure 14 — Gangaram Pabuch Sirmauri sāñcā running page — strong manuscript-layout evidence

Text type: Sirmauri ritual/calendar running text

  • Upper image is a running manuscript page; the lower table from the proposal is omitted here.
  • Lines are ruled and filled across most of the writing area.
  • Daṇḍā-like punctuation and sectioning occur, but line breaks still often follow the physical line rather than a visible word-space system.

Line-break implication: Good primary evidence for manuscript wrapping and punctuation in Sirmauri running text.

VL No cluster split

Figure 16 — Gangaram Pabuch Sirmauri running page — strong manuscript-layout evidence

Text type: Sirmauri ritual/calendar running text

  • The diagrammatic upper image is omitted; this card uses the lower running page.
  • Several lines end with punctuation-like signs, but others simply end at the ruled margin.
  • The text has ligature-like/cursive stroke continuity, but no evidence of breaking inside a base+vowel-sign cluster.

Line-break implication: Useful for distinguishing punctuation-driven breaks from physical line-fill breaks.

VL No cluster split

Figure 17a — Gangaram Pabuch Sirmauri sāñcā page — strong manuscript-layout evidence

Text type: Sirmauri ritual/calendar running text

  • Repeated boxed section markers at the left and long ruled lines to the right.
  • Some line endings are associated with punctuation/section structure; others are physical line ends.
  • The page demonstrates that a line-break analysis must keep section signs separate from ordinary text wrapping.

Line-break implication: Strong for ruled-line behavior; segmentation still needed before claiming lexical word-internal defaults.

VL No cluster split

Figure 17b — Gangaram Pabuch Sirmauri running page — strong manuscript-layout evidence

Text type: Sirmauri ritual/calendar running text

  • A clear running manuscript page with broad ruled lines and relatively dense writing.
  • Text fills the available width; visible word spacing is weak or absent.
  • Line ends are not accompanied by a dedicated hyphen/continuation mark.

Line-break implication: One of the better sāñcā witnesses for the general physical line-fill pattern.

VL No cluster split

Figure 18 — tower inscription — moderate inscription evidence

Text type: inscription on physical surface

  • Text is constrained by the physical wooden/tower surface rather than paper rules.
  • The inscription is useful as non-paper evidence of line arrangement, but image quality and surface damage limit segmentation.
  • No break inside a combining-mark cluster is visible.

Line-break implication: Supplementary evidence only; not a primary basis for word-breaking defaults.

VL No cluster split

Figure 35 — full running page with variant ca and ī — strong manuscript-layout evidence

Text type: running manuscript page

  • Full page of running text, included here because it is not just a table/chart.
  • Ruled line widths determine wrapping; visible spaces do not form a reliable word-boundary system.
  • Useful for checking letter variants while also supporting the same line-fill pattern.

Line-break implication: Good additional witness after Figures 6–17; still requires fluent reading for lexical boundary classification.

VL No cluster split

Sources

L2 proposal: Proposal to encode Sirmauri in Unicode (L2/25-224)

Grierson source used for Figures 2 and 4: Linguistic Survey of India, Vol. IX, Part IV: Specimens of Pahari Languages and Gujuri
